lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Go, captain, from me greet the Danish king;

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Tell him that, by his license, Fortinbras

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Craves the conveyance of a promised march

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Over his kingdom. You know the rendezvous.

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: If that his majesty would aught with us,

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: We shall express our duty in his eye;

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: And let him know so.

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: I will do’t, my lord.

lines.speaker: PRINCE FORTINBRAS
lines.text: Go softly on.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Good sir, whose powers are these?

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: They are of Norway, sir.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How purposed, sir, I pray you?

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: Against some part of Poland.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Who commands them, sir?

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: The nephews to old Norway, Fortinbras.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Goes it against the main of Poland, sir,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Or for some frontier?

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: Truly to speak, and with no addition,

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: We go to gain a little patch of ground

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: That hath in it no profit but the name.

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: To pay five ducats, five, I would not farm it;

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: Nor will it yield to Norway or the Pole

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: A ranker rate, should it be sold in fee.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, then the Polack never will defend it.

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: Yes, it is already garrison’d.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Two thousand souls and twenty thousand ducats

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Will not debate the question of this straw:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: This is the imposthume of much wealth and peace,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That inward breaks, and shows no cause without

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why the man dies. I humbly thank you, sir.

lines.speaker: CAPTAIN
lines.text: God be wi’ you, sir.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Will’t please you go, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I’ll be with you straight. Go a little before.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: How all occasions do inform against me,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And spur my dull revenge! What is a man,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: If his chief good and market of his time

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Be but to sleep and feed? a beast, no more.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sure, he that made us with such large discourse,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Looking before and after, gave us not

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That capability and god-like reason

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To fust in us unused. Now, whether it be

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Bestial oblivion, or some craven scruple

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Of thinking too precisely on the event,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A thought which, quarter’d, hath but one part wisdom

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And ever three parts coward, I do not know

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why yet I live to say “This thing’s to do;”

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Sith I have cause and will and strength and means

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To do’t. Examples gross as earth exhort me:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Witness this army of such mass and charge

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Led by a delicate and tender prince,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Whose spirit with divine ambition puff’d

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Makes mouths at the invisible event,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Exposing what is mortal and unsure

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To all that fortune, death and danger dare,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Even for an egg-shell. Rightly to be great

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Is not to stir without great argument,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But greatly to find quarrel in a straw

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: When honour’s at the stake. How stand I then,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That have a father kill’d, a mother stain’d,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Excitements of my reason and my blood

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And let all sleep? while, to my shame, I see

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The imminent death of twenty thousand men,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That, for a fantasy and trick of fame,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Go to their graves like beds, fight for a plot

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Whereon the numbers cannot try the cause,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Which is not tomb enough and continent

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To hide the slain? O, from this time forth,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My thoughts be bloody, or be nothing worth!

stage_directions.text: Enter Fortinbras, a Captain, and Soldiers, marching.
Enter Hamlet, Rosencrantz, Guildenstern, and others.

glosses.phrase: Two thousand souls and twenty thousand ducats
chief good and market of his time
glosses.definition: best aim and profit of his life
intellect and divine power of thought

glosses.phrase: thinking too precisely on the event
glosses.definition: reflecting too minutely on the outcome

glosses.phrase: greatly to find quarrel in a straw
glosses.definition: nobly to take offense at a trifle

glosses.phrase: my thoughts be bloody, or be nothing worth
glosses.definition: let my thoughts be violent, or else worthless

annotations.title: Fortinbras's army and the politics of honor
annotations.body: Fortinbras appears briefly en route to Poland, his army marching to fight over a patch of ground Hamlet's captain dismisses as 'the plot whereon the numbers cannot try the cause.' Shakespeare stages the man against whom Hamlet has been measured all along: a prince whose father was killed by King Hamlet, and who has organized an entire military campaign to recover the family honor. Hamlet's whole strategic horizon — delay, interrogation, doubt — has no corollary in Fortinbras. The scene poses the play's sharpest question: is Fortinbras's decisive honor-seeking noble, or is it the waste of twenty thousand lives over an eggshell? The final soliloquy will refuse to settle it.
sources: Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)

annotations.title: 'How all occasions do inform against me'
annotations.body: Hamlet's final soliloquy — absent from the Folio but present in the Second Quarto — is the prince's most thorough self-examination. 'What is a man, / If his chief good and market of his time / Be but to sleep and feed?' echoes the earlier 'what is this quintessence of dust?' but turns the question from species to self. The speech's climax — 'Rightly to be great / Is not to stir without great argument, / But greatly to find quarrel in a straw / When honour's at the stake' — is both a critique of Fortinbras and an envy of him. The closing couplet, 'O, from this time forth, / My thoughts be bloody, or be nothing worth,' is the play's last soliloquy of resolution, and the next time we see Hamlet he will already be different.

annotations.title: The soliloquy's textual ghost
annotations.body: This entire soliloquy (about 35 lines) is missing from the 1623 First Folio. Many editors assume Shakespeare's company cut it in later performance for length; some scholars, including Paul Werstine and Barbara Mowat, argue the cut was thematic — the Folio Hamlet is a less meditative figure, closer to a man of action. Whichever theory is right, modern editions typically restore the speech from the Second Quarto because it is a pivot point for the character. Without it, Hamlet's decisiveness in Act 5 — the letter forgery, the pirate-ship escape, the readiness for the duel — arrives unearned.
sources: Paul Werstine, 'The Textual Mystery of Hamlet' (1988)
Barbara Mowat and Paul Werstine, eds., Folger Shakespeare Library edition

trials.prompt: Where is Fortinbras leading his army in this scene?
options: To reclaim his father's lost lands in Denmark.
To fight the Poles over a worthless patch of ground.
To invade England with the support of Denmark.
To assist the King of Norway against a rebellion.

trials.prompt: What does the Captain admit about the campaign?
options: That the Poles will surrender without a fight.
That the land is not worth five ducats to farm, yet thousands will die for it.
That Fortinbras is a secret agent of Claudius.
That the soldiers will mutiny before reaching Poland.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet's final soliloquy contrast his own behavior with?
options: The courage of Fortinbras, who acts decisively for mere honor while Hamlet delays.
The piety of Claudius, whose prayers Hamlet cannot match.
The loyalty of Rosencrantz and Guildenstern.
The cunning of Polonius, whose methods he admires.

trials.prompt: What does Hamlet mean by 'Rightly to be great / Is not to stir without great argument, / But greatly to find quarrel in a straw'?
options: True greatness avoids all conflict.
True greatness can turn a trifle into a cause of honor when honor is at stake.
Nobles should not quarrel over farmland.
Ambition is always a petty motive.

trials.prompt: What vow does Hamlet make at the end of the soliloquy?
options: To return to Wittenberg and pursue his studies.
That his thoughts, from this moment, must be bloody or worthless.
To spare Claudius for his mother's sake.
To reveal all to Horatio.
